content.text:
<TOPIC_NOTE>
# US Iran War

## One-Line Summary
Since February 28, 2026, the United States and Israel have been in open armed conflict with Iran; Iran’s Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei was killed in the opening strikes, and the U.S. is now enforcing a naval blockade of Iranian ports while a fragile, repeatedly extended ceasefire coexists with sporadic fire and a Strait of Hormuz crisis that has triggered the IEA-described “largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market,” with major implications for India’s energy security and maritime trade. ([apnews.com](https://apnews.com/article/8de8054f3abd4688f894c657467ee3dd?utm_source=openai))

## Why This Matters
- India imports ~88–89% of its crude oil; most India-bound energy cargoes normally transit the Strait of Hormuz. Disruptions since March 2026 have stranded Indian/India-bound ships and raised global energy prices. ([indianexpress.com](https://indianexpress.com/article/explained/explained-economics/indias-reliance-on-imported-oil-may-hit-fresh-high-in-fy26-10548802/?utm_source=openai))
- The U.S. blockade and Iran’s counter-measures have created a dual-restriction environment at Hormuz, directly affecting India’s energy inflows, insurance costs, and shipping risk. ([cfr.org](https://www.cfr.org/articles/united-states-blockades-strait-of-hormuz-after-u-s-iran-talks/?utm_source=openai))
- India has a 10‑year Chabahar port contract (signed May 13, 2024) but the U.S. revoked the 2018 Chabahar sanctions exception in September 2025, later granting a temporary carve‑out only until April 26, 2026—raising sanctions-exposure risk for Indian entities. ([pib.gov.in](https://pib.gov.in/PressReleasePage.aspx?PRID=2020454&utm_source=openai))

## Current State
Facts (as of May 10, 2026):
- Start and character of the war: U.S. and Israel launched major strikes on Iran on February 28, 2026 (Operation Epic Fury), and Iran responded with missile/drone salvos; Iran’s Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei was killed that day. ([centcom.mil](https://www.centcom.mil/MEDIA/STATEMENTS/Statements-View/Article/4418396/us-forces-launch-operation-epic-fury/?utm_source=openai))
- Naval blockade: CENTCOM announced an impartial blockade of ships entering/exiting Iranian ports effective April 13, 2026; multiple Iranian-flagged tankers have been disabled enforcing the blockade. ([centcom.mil](https://www.centcom.mil/MEDIA/PRESS-RELEASES/Press-Release-View/Article/4457255/us-to-blockade-ships-entering-or-exiting-iranian-ports/))
- Strait of Hormuz: Iran intermittently closed or restricted Hormuz; the U.S. launched “Project Freedom” (from May 4) to “guide” merchant ships while maintaining the blockade; ceasefire claims coexist with exchanges of fire and mine-clearance operations. ([centcom.mil](https://www.centcom.mil/MEDIA/PRESS-RELEASES/Press-Release-View/Article/4476318/us-military-supports-launch-of-project-freedom-in-strait-of-hormuz/))
- Diplomacy: A conditional ceasefire was arranged on April 8 and extended, but Islamabad talks on April 11–12 failed; Council dynamics are split (UNSC Res. 2817 adopted Mar 11 condemning Iranian regional attacks; a later Hormuz resolution effort failed on Apr 7 due to vetoes). ([commonslibrary.parliament.uk](https://commonslibrary.parliament.uk/research-briefings/cbp-10637/))
- Nuclear file: IAEA Director General Grossi said on March 2 there was “no indication” nuclear facilities were hit in initial strikes; he urged strict verification in any end‑of‑war deal. ([investing.com](https://www.investing.com/news/world-news/no-sign-irans-nuclear-sites-were-hit-iaea-says-but-iran-alleges-one-was-4533937?utm_source=openai))
- India-specific: DG Shipping issued multiple advisories (from Feb 28 onward); MEA has advised Indians to leave Iran and avoid travel; Indian Navy is actively escorting vessels under Operation Sankalp; at least 22 India-bound ships and 611 seafarers were reported stranded west of Hormuz in mid‑March. ([dgshipping.gov.in](https://www.dgshipping.gov.in/WriteReadData/News/202602280342249592926MARSECDGSCircular08of2026dated28Feb20262.pdf?utm_source=openai))

Interpretation:
- The blockade/escort “dual regime” at Hormuz has moved risk from random interdiction to systematic state-on-state naval enforcement, compressing insurance capacity and deterring transits even when windows open. ([csis.org](https://www.csis.org/analysis/strait-hormuz-8-charts?utm_source=openai))

Provisional/volatile items:
- Ceasefire status and scope, numbers of successful transits during any “open” periods, and the terms of any draft end‑of‑war memo remain fluid; follow official communiqués and IMO/UKMTO notices. ([apnews.com](https://apnews.com/article/2749dc877f0ac34a0ccd4f0530786009?utm_source=openai))

## Historical Context
- 1979–2015: Cycles of confrontation, culminating in JCPOA (2015). 
- 2018–2024: U.S. exit from JCPOA; escalating proxy attacks; Jan 28, 2024 Tower 22 drone attack (Jordan) killed 3 U.S. soldiers; U.S. retaliatory strikes Feb 2, 2024. ([centcom.mil](https://www.centcom.mil/MEDIA/PRESS-RELEASES/Press-Release-View/Article/3658552/update-us-casualties-in-northeast-jordan-near-syrian-border/?utm_source=openai))
- 2024–2025: Iran‑Israel direct strikes (2024) and subsequent escalations set conditions for the 2026 war (as documented by parliamentary/think-tank timelines). ([commonslibrary.parliament.uk](https://commonslibrary.parliament.uk/research-briefings/cbp-10637/))
- Precedent: The 1980s “Tanker War” saw U.S. convoying and clashes (Earnest Will/Praying Mantis), but shipping did not largely halt as it has in 2026. ([congress.gov](https://www.congress.gov/crs-product/R45281?utm_source=openai))

## Key Actors and Institutions
- United States: President/White House; DoD/CENTCOM/5th Fleet; War Powers oversight; State Department. ([centcom.mil](https://www.centcom.mil/MEDIA/STATEMENTS/Statements-View/Article/4418396/us-forces-launch-operation-epic-fury/?utm_source=openai))
- Iran: President, IRGC Navy, Artesh; MFA; Iran’s UN Mission. ([digitallibrary.un.org](https://digitallibrary.un.org/record/4109006?utm_source=openai))
- Israel: Political/military leadership coordinating initial strikes (Feb 28). ([axios.com](https://www.axios.com/2026/02/28/iran-us-israel-strikes-operation-epic-fury?utm_source=openai))
- Multilaterals/regulators: UN Security Council; IAEA; IMO; UKMTO; IEA. ([digitallibrary.un.org](https://digitallibrary.un.org/record/4105560?utm_source=openai))
- India: MEA; Directorate General of Shipping; Indian Navy (Western Naval Command) and Operation Sankalp; Ministry of Ports/India Ports Global Ltd (Chabahar); MoPNG/PPAC; ISPRL. ([mea.gov.in](https://www.mea.gov.in/iran-travel-advisory.htm?utm_source=openai))

## Relevant Policies, Laws, and Programs
- U.S. sanctions and authorities: IFCA; IEEPA; IRGC FTO designation; 2018 Chabahar exception revoked Sep 16, 2025; temporary Treasury letter shielding Chabahar activities until Apr 26, 2026. ([dentons.com](https://www.dentons.com/en/insights/alerts/2025/september/17/maximum-pressure?utm_source=openai))
- UNSC Resolution 2817 (Mar 11, 2026) condemning Iranian attacks on regional states; subsequent draft on Hormuz security vetoed (Apr 7). ([digitallibrary.un.org](https://digitallibrary.un.org/record/4105560?utm_source=openai))
- Law of naval warfare and blockades (San Remo/London Naval Conference principles) underpin legality debates about U.S. blockade and Iran’s Hormuz closure claims. ([britannica.com](https://www.britannica.com/topic/blockade-warfare?utm_source=openai))
- India’s maritime security measures: DGS Circulars (Feb 28 onward) for Hormuz/Persian Gulf; Operation Sankalp escorts since 2019 (intensified 2026). ([dgshipping.gov.in](https://www.dgshipping.gov.in/WriteReadData/News/202602280342249592926MARSECDGSCircular08of2026dated28Feb20262.pdf?utm_source=openai))
- India’s Chabahar long‑term contract (May 13, 2024). ([pib.gov.in](https://pib.gov.in/PressReleasePage.aspx?PRID=2020454&utm_source=openai))

## Data and Indicators
- Energy chokepoint baseline: ~21 mb/d of oil (≈20–21% of global liquids) and significant LNG volumes normally transit Hormuz. ([eia.gov](https://www.eia.gov/todayinenergy/detail.php?id=39932&os=avdavdxhup&utm_source=openai))
- Disruption magnitude: IEA characterizes the present shock as the “largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market.” ([iea.org](https://www.iea.org/reports/sheltering-from-oil-shocks/introduction-and-context?utm_source=openai))
- Price signals: Brent touched ~$112/bbl in early April amid closures; volatility remains elevated. (Data consolidated from IEA/market summaries.) ([iea.org](https://www.iea.org/reports/oil-market-report-march-2026?ftag=MSFd61514f&utm_source=openai))
- India’s oil import dependence: ~88–89% in FY25–FY26 (PPAC-cited reporting). ([indianexpress.com](https://indianexpress.com/article/explained/explained-economics/indias-reliance-on-imported-oil-may-hit-fresh-high-in-fy26-10548802/?utm_source=openai))
- India’s SPR: 5.33 MMT capacity; ~64% filled as of March 23, 2026—about 9.5 days of cover at 100% capacity. ([indianexpress.com](https://indianexpress.com/article/explained/explained-economics/india-strategic-petroleum-reserve-capacity-west-asia-10597131/?utm_source=openai))
- Indian shipping exposure: 22 India‑bound vessels and 611 seafarers stranded west of Hormuz reported mid‑March; Indian Navy escorts ongoing. ([ndtv.com](https://www.ndtv.com/india-news/how-indian-navy-ships-are-escorting-vessels-amid-strait-of-hormuz-closure-shivalik-nanda-devi-jag-laadki-iran-war-gulf-of-oman-aden-11226419?pfrom=home-ndtv_topscroll_Imagetopscroll))
- U.S. blockade enforcement actions: multiple Iranian-flagged tankers disabled or diverted (e.g., May 6 and May 8 incidents). ([centcom.mil](https://www.centcom.mil/MEDIA/PRESS-RELEASES/Press-Release-View/Article/4480820/us-disables-2-more-vessels-violating-blockade-in-gulf-of-oman/))

Data gaps:
- Verified totals for successful convoyed transits by flag since April 17.
- Full quantification of insurance premiums/war‑risk surcharges faced by Indian carriers.

## Strengths
- Indian Navy presence and experience (Sankalp) in the Gulf enhances ability to shepherd India-bound cargoes during partial openings. ([pib.gov.in](https://www.pib.gov.in/Pressreleaseshare.aspx?PRID=1835382&utm_source=openai))
- Diversified crude slate post‑2022 (incl. Russia) offers some flexibility, though Hormuz shock dominates regional flows. ([dgciskol.gov.in](https://www.dgciskol.gov.in/writereaddata/Downloads/20251024153605Insights%20into%20Import%20of%20Crude%20Oil%20and%20International%20Crude%20Oil%20prices%20.pdf?utm_source=openai))
- Strategic clarity on Chabahar as an Indian anchor on Iran’s coast (contracted), potentially important post‑war. ([pib.gov.in](https://pib.gov.in/PressReleasePage.aspx?PRID=2020454&utm_source=openai))

## Bottlenecks
- High import dependence on crude and LNG; limited SPR cover compared with duration/intensity of disruption. ([indianexpress.com](https://indianexpress.com/article/explained/explained-economics/indias-reliance-on-imported-oil-may-hit-fresh-high-in-fy26-10548802/?utm_source=openai))
- Sanctions risk to Indian public/private entities operating near Iran (Chabahar/port services/insurance) after U.S. waiver revocation. ([dentons.com](https://www.dentons.com/en/insights/alerts/2025/september/17/maximum-pressure?utm_source=openai))
- Legal/operational uncertainty at Hormuz with overlapping ceasefire, blockade, and Iranian enforcement claims deters shippers and insurers. ([ungeneva.org](https://www.ungeneva.org/en/news-media/news/2026/04/117441/security-council-russia-and-china-veto-resolution-strait-hormuz?utm_source=openai))

## Global Comparison
- Unlike the 1980s Tanker War, the 2026 crisis produced a near-halt in transits for extended periods due to mines, drones, and insurance paralysis, magnifying macroeconomic impact. ([congress.gov](https://www.congress.gov/crs-product/R45281?utm_source=openai))

## Opportunities
- Shape and join multilateral maritime safety mechanisms around Hormuz (with IMO/UNSC text where feasible) to improve convoy predictability for India‑bound vessels. ([ungeneva.org](https://www.ungeneva.org/en/news-media/news/2026/04/118024/chokepoints-and-conflict-how-hormuz-crisis-exposing-global-shipping?utm_source=openai))
- Accelerate SPR phase‑II capacity and policy frameworks to leverage commercial+strategic stocks. ([pib.gov.in](https://www.pib.gov.in/Pressreleaseshare.aspx?PRID=2113233&utm_source=openai))
- Use India’s equities at Chabahar and in the INSTC to position for post‑war regional logistics re‑wiring—contingent on sanctions compliance. ([pib.gov.in](https://pib.gov.in/PressReleasePage.aspx?PRID=2020454&utm_source=openai))

## Risks
- Prolonged blockade/closure dynamics at Hormuz could pressure India’s current account, rupee, and inflation; domestic price‑management measures have fiscal costs. ([business-standard.com](https://www.business-standard.com/economy/news/government-cuts-fuel-excise-duty-imposes-export-levy-on-diesel-atf-126032700327_1.html?utm_source=openai))
- Escalation channels (Lebanon/Hezbollah, Yemen/Red Sea) could expand maritime risk envelope for India‑flagged ships. ([commonslibrary.parliament.uk](https://commonslibrary.parliament.uk/research-briefings/cbp-10637/))
- Regulatory exposure if U.S. sanctions tighten or secondary sanctions extend to counterparties supporting India‑linked flows via Iran. ([dentons.com](https://www.dentons.com/en/insights/alerts/2025/september/17/maximum-pressure?utm_source=openai))

## Future Scenarios
- Negotiated endgame (most benign): A short “one‑page” framework—nuclear constraints, Hormuz reopening, phased sanctions relief—leads to demobilization and insurance normalization in Q3–Q4 2026. This remains uncertain. ([axios.com](https://www.axios.com/2026/05/06/iran-us-deal-one-page-memo?utm_source=openai))
- Prolonged standoff: Ceasefire holds on paper while U.S. blockade + Iranian counter‑pressure persist; sporadic violence, partial ship “windows,” elevated premiums; India continues expensive end‑around sourcing and naval escorts. ([cfr.org](https://www.cfr.org/articles/united-states-blockades-strait-of-hormuz-after-u-s-iran-talks/?utm_source=openai))
- Renewed escalation: Talks fail, large strikes resume (energy infrastructure/bridges threatened), prompting broader regional spillover and deeper oil/gas supply shock. ([commonslibrary.parliament.uk](https://commonslibrary.parliament.uk/research-briefings/cbp-10637/))

## What India Should Watch Next
- Any formal text on a U.S.–Iran ceasefire/end‑of‑war memorandum and explicit Hormuz reopening timelines; monitor UNSC drafts on maritime security. ([ungeneva.org](https://www.ungeneva.org/en/news-media/news/2026/05/118421/bahrain-and-us-float-security-council-resolution-strait-hormuz?utm_source=openai))
- CENTCOM operational notices and IMO/UKMTO advisories for convoying windows and mine‑clearance progress. ([centcom.mil](https://www.centcom.mil/MEDIA/PRESS-RELEASES/Press-Release-View/Article/4457220/us-forces-start-mine-clearance-mission-in-strait-of-hormuz/?utm_source=openai))
- U.S. sanctions posture toward Chabahar after April 26, 2026 Treasury carve‑out expiry; any renewed waivers or enforcement actions. ([sansad.in](https://sansad.in/getFile/loksabhaquestions/annex/186/AU1103_r6YhID.pdf?source=pqals))
- IEA OMR and World Bank/IMF updates on price shocks; OPEC+ supply adjustments. ([iea.org](https://www.iea.org/reports/oil-market-report-march-2026?ftag=MSFd61514f&utm_source=openai))
- DGS/MEA advisories for Indian seafarers and cargoes; Indian Navy escort footprints. ([dgshipping.gov.in](https://www.dgshipping.gov.in/WriteReadData/News/202602280342249592926MARSECDGSCircular08of2026dated28Feb20262.pdf?utm_source=openai))

## Open Questions
- Will Project Freedom evolve into a durable escorted corridor acceptable to both sides, or remain a stop‑start operation vulnerable to incident? ([centcom.mil](https://www.centcom.mil/MEDIA/PRESS-RELEASES/Press-Release-View/Article/4476318/us-military-supports-launch-of-project-freedom-in-strait-of-hormuz/))
- Can a nuclear verification regime acceptable to both Washington and Tehran be embedded soon enough to lift sanctions and normalize trade? ([apnews.com](https://apnews.com/article/f96d4f5f5a0b086fec5ddba158a36145?utm_source=openai))
- What is the post‑war sanctioning environment for Chabahar/IPGL operations and for Indian shipping/insurance pools engaging Iran‑adjacent trades? ([sansad.in](https://sansad.in/getFile/loksabhaquestions/annex/186/AU1103_r6YhID.pdf?source=pqals))

## Sources
- CENTCOM press releases/statements (Apr–May 2026) on blockade, Project Freedom, Epic Fury. ([centcom.mil](https://www.centcom.mil/MEDIA/PRESS-RELEASES/Press-Release-View/Article/4457255/us-to-blockade-ships-entering-or-exiting-iranian-ports/))
- White House releases on Operation Epic Fury objectives and progress (March–April 2026). ([whitehouse.gov](https://www.whitehouse.gov/releases/2026/04/peace-through-strength-operation-epic-fury-crushes-iranian-threat-as-ceasefire-takes-hold/?utm_source=openai))
- AP/Reuters/Axios/BBC/Le Monde coverage for war onset, Khamenei’s death, and Hormuz diplomacy. ([apnews.com](https://apnews.com/article/8de8054f3abd4688f894c657467ee3dd?utm_source=openai))
- UN digital library documents: UNSC Resolution 2817 (Mar 11, 2026); Iran letter on U.S. blockade (Apr 13, 2026). ([digitallibrary.un.org](https://digitallibrary.un.org/record/4105560?utm_source=openai))
- IEA Oil/Gas reports and statements on supply disruption scale (Mar–Apr 2026). ([iea.org](https://www.iea.org/reports/oil-market-report-march-2026?ftag=MSFd61514f&utm_source=openai))
- India: DG Shipping circulars (Feb–Mar 2026), MEA travel advisories (Apr 7), Operation Sankalp reporting (NDTV/PIB), Chabahar contract (PIB May 13, 2024) and Lok Sabha Q&A on Chabahar waiver revocation/Treasury letter. ([dgshipping.gov.in](https://www.dgshipping.gov.in/WriteReadData/News/202602280342249592926MARSECDGSCircular08of2026dated28Feb20262.pdf?utm_source=openai))

<BRIEF>
# Brief: US Iran War

## Bottom Line
The U.S.–Iran war that began on February 28, 2026 has produced simultaneous ceasefire diplomacy, a U.S. naval blockade of Iranian ports, and a contested Strait of Hormuz. The IEA calls the energy shock the largest in history. India—highly dependent on Gulf energy and with equities in Chabahar—faces sustained supply, sanctions, and maritime‑risk exposure. ([centcom.mil](https://www.centcom.mil/MEDIA/PRESS-RELEASES/Press-Release-View/Article/4457255/us-to-blockade-ships-entering-or-exiting-iranian-ports/))

## Key Takeaways
- The U.S. blockade (from April 13) and Iran’s counter‑measures have created a “dual restriction” at Hormuz, intermittently punctuated by U.S. escort operations (“Project Freedom”). ([centcom.mil](https://www.centcom.mil/MEDIA/PRESS-RELEASES/Press-Release-View/Article/4457255/us-to-blockade-ships-entering-or-exiting-iranian-ports/))
- India’s exposure is acute: ~88–89% crude import dependence, thin SPR (~5.33 MMT, ~64% filled), and dozens of India‑bound ships affected; the Navy is escorting under Op Sankalp. ([indianexpress.com](https://indianexpress.com/article/explained/explained-economics/indias-reliance-on-imported-oil-may-hit-fresh-high-in-fy26-10548802/?utm_source=openai))
- Chabahar remains strategic but sanctions risk rose after the U.S. revoked the 2018 waiver; a temporary Treasury letter covered activity only until April 26, 2026. ([sansad.in](https://sansad.in/getFile/loksabhaquestions/annex/186/AU1103_r6YhID.pdf?source=pqals))

## Strategic Context
- Epic Fury’s initial strikes killed Khamenei and hit Iranian military infrastructure; Iran retaliated region‑wide; UNSC 2817 condemned Iranian regional attacks; a Hormuz security resolution later failed due to vetoes. ([apnews.com](https://apnews.com/article/8de8054f3abd4688f894c657467ee3dd?utm_source=openai))
- IAEA reports no initial nuclear‑site damage but insists any end‑of‑war deal must embed robust verification. ([investing.com](https://www.investing.com/news/world-news/no-sign-irans-nuclear-sites-were-hit-iaea-says-but-iran-alleges-one-was-4533937?utm_source=openai))

## Evidence
- CENTCOM blockade and enforcement releases (Apr–May 2026). ([centcom.mil](https://www.centcom.mil/MEDIA/PRESS-RELEASES/Press-Release-View/Article/4457255/us-to-blockade-ships-entering-or-exiting-iranian-ports/))
- Project Freedom launch details (May 3–4). ([centcom.mil](https://www.centcom.mil/MEDIA/PRESS-RELEASES/Press-Release-View/Article/4476318/us-military-supports-launch-of-project-freedom-in-strait-of-hormuz/))
- IEA OMR statements (Mar–Apr 2026). ([iea.org](https://www.iea.org/reports/oil-market-report-march-2026?ftag=MSFd61514f&utm_source=openai))
- UK House of Commons briefing on ceasefire/talks (Apr 24). ([commonslibrary.parliament.uk](https://commonslibrary.parliament.uk/research-briefings/cbp-10637/))
- NDTV on Indian Navy escorts/stranded ships (Mar 17). ([ndtv.com](https://www.ndtv.com/india-news/how-indian-navy-ships-are-escorting-vessels-amid-strait-of-hormuz-closure-shivalik-nanda-devi-jag-laadki-iran-war-gulf-of-oman-aden-11226419?pfrom=home-ndtv_topscroll_Imagetopscroll))
- Lok Sabha Q&A on Chabahar waiver revocation/temporary cover (Dec 5, 2025). ([sansad.in](https://sansad.in/getFile/loksabhaquestions/annex/186/AU1103_r6YhID.pdf?source=pqals))

## Implications
- Energy: Expect prolonged premium on oil/LNG delivered to India until Hormuz fully normalizes; OMC under‑recoveries and fiscal measures likely persist. ([business-standard.com](https://www.business-standard.com/economy/news/government-cuts-fuel-excise-duty-imposes-export-levy-on-diesel-atf-126032700327_1.html?utm_source=openai))
- Maritime security: Indian Navy convoying and granular DGS routing/advisories will be essential for risk management during partial openings. ([dgshipping.gov.in](https://www.dgshipping.gov.in/WriteReadData/News/202602280342249592926MARSECDGSCircular08of2026dated28Feb20262.pdf?utm_source=openai))
- Sanctions compliance: Post‑April 26 exposure at Chabahar and Iran‑adjacent shipping insurance must be reassessed; seek legal clarity/comfort letters where possible. ([sansad.in](https://sansad.in/getFile/loksabhaquestions/annex/186/AU1103_r6YhID.pdf?source=pqals))

## Recommended Next Research
- Map cargo flows and feasible “bypass” routings (e.g., via Red Sea/East Africa) for Indian energy imports, with cost/time deltas versus normal Hormuz lanes.
- Compile India‑specific insurance/war‑risk pricing data for tankers and gas carriers; compare India‑flag vs FoC.
- Track any renewed U.S. waivers or General Licenses affecting Chabahar or Indian shipping/insurance services.
- Build a daily Hormuz dashboard (UKMTO/IMO/CENTCOM/JWC) for convoy windows, mine‑clearance status, and incident logs. ([centcom.mil](https://www.centcom.mil/MEDIA/PRESS-RELEASES/Press-Release-View/Article/4457220/us-forces-start-mine-clearance-mission-in-strait-of-hormuz/?utm_source=openai))
</BRIEF>

<PRESENTATION_OUTLINE>
# Presentation Outline: US Iran War

## Audience
Indian policy leadership (MEA, MoPNG, Navy, IPGL), energy and shipping regulators, and risk officers in OMCs/insurers.

## Core Message
A U.S.–Iran war plus a U.S. blockade and Iran’s counter‑closure at Hormuz have created a structurally risky maritime regime; India must manage near‑term energy security and sanctions exposure while preparing for post‑war logistics re‑wiring.

## Slide Outline
For each slide, write:
1. Claim title
   - Proof object:
   - Evidence:
   - Source:

1. War began Feb 28, 2026; Iran’s Supreme Leader killed
   - Proof object: Opening-day reporting and official statements
   - Evidence: Coordinated U.S.–Israeli strikes; AP/Reuters confirm Khamenei killed
   - Source: ([apnews.com](https://apnews.com/article/8de8054f3abd4688f894c657467ee3dd?utm_source=openai))

2. U.S. naval blockade declared Apr 12; enforcement ongoing
   - Proof object: CENTCOM press release
   - Evidence: Impartial blockade of ships entering/exiting Iranian ports; effective Apr 13
   - Source: ([centcom.mil](https://www.centcom.mil/MEDIA/PRESS-RELEASES/Press-Release-View/Article/4457255/us-to-blockade-ships-entering-or-exiting-iranian-ports/))

3. Project Freedom escorts coexist with blockade and intermittent fire
   - Proof object: CENTCOM/press notices
   - Evidence: U.S. began “guiding” ships through Hormuz; skirmishes and mine-clearance reported
   - Source: ([centcom.mil](https://www.centcom.mil/MEDIA/PRESS-RELEASES/Press-Release-View/Article/4476318/us-military-supports-launch-of-project-freedom-in-strait-of-hormuz/))

4. Ceasefire diplomacy: talks held; UNSC split
   - Proof object: HoC Library brief; UNSC records
   - Evidence: Conditional ceasefire (from Apr 8) with Islamabad talks; Res. 2817 adopted; later Hormuz draft vetoed
   - Source: ([commonslibrary.parliament.uk](https://commonslibrary.parliament.uk/research-briefings/cbp-10637/))

5. IAEA: no initial nuclear-site damage; verification essential
   - Proof object: IAEA DG statement
   - Evidence: “No indication” of damage (Mar 2); calls for robust checks in any deal
   - Source: ([investing.com](https://www.investing.com/news/world-news/no-sign-irans-nuclear-sites-were-hit-iaea-says-but-iran-alleges-one-was-4533937?utm_source=openai))

6. Hormuz disruption is historic in scale
   - Proof object: IEA OMR/analysis
   - Evidence: “Largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market”
   - Source: ([iea.org](https://www.iea.org/reports/sheltering-from-oil-shocks/introduction-and-context?utm_source=openai))

7. India’s exposure: dependency, SPR, stranded ships
   - Proof object: PPAC/ISPRL/NDTV
   - Evidence: ~88–89% import dependence; SPR 5.33 MMT (~64% filled); 22 India‑bound ships and 611 seafarers stranded mid‑March; Navy escorts
   - Source: ([indianexpress.com](https://indianexpress.com/article/explained/explained-economics/indias-reliance-on-imported-oil-may-hit-fresh-high-in-fy26-10548802/?utm_source=openai))

8. Chabahar: strategic stake amid sanctions headwinds
   - Proof object: PIB (contract); Lok Sabha Q&A (waiver revocation/temporary cover)
   - Evidence: 10‑year contract (May 13, 2024); U.S. revoked 2018 exception; temporary cover to Apr 26, 2026
   - Source: ([pib.gov.in](https://pib.gov.in/PressReleasePage.aspx?PRID=2020454&utm_source=openai))

9. Legal frame: blockade/closure under LOAC and UNCLOS principles
   - Proof object: RUSI/Chatham analyses; San Remo background
   - Evidence: Declaration/effectiveness/impartiality tests; Iran’s closure claims scrutinized
   - Source: ([rusi.org](https://www.rusi.org/explore-our-research/publications/commentary/us-blockade-iran-how-it-might-function-or-not?utm_source=openai))

10. Macro impact: prices, insurance, logistics
   - Proof object: IEA OMR; market/insurance commentary
   - Evidence: Brent spikes; AIS-based partial openings; insurers’ risk aversion
   - Source: ([iea.org](https://www.iea.org/reports/oil-market-report-march-2026?ftag=MSFd61514f&utm_source=openai))

11. India’s immediate posture: escorts + price management
   - Proof object: DGS/MEA notices; policy measures
   - Evidence: Routing/crew advisories; excise adjustments; price holds
   - Source: ([dgshipping.gov.in](https://www.dgshipping.gov.in/WriteReadData/News/202602280342249592926MARSECDGSCircular08of2026dated28Feb20262.pdf?utm_source=openai))

12. Paths ahead: deal, standoff, or escalation
   - Proof object: Negotiation reporting
   - Evidence: “One‑page memo” possibility vs prolonged dual-restriction vs renewed strikes
   - Source: ([axios.com](https://www.axios.com/2026/05/06/iran-us-deal-one-page-memo?utm_source=openai))

## Visual Ideas
- Conflict timeline (Feb 28–May 10) overlaying ceasefire windows and blockade milestones.
- Actor map (U.S./Israel vs Iran; UN/IMO/IEA; India’s institutions).
- Hormuz traffic trendline (pre‑war baseline vs March–April partial transits).
- India risk dashboard: dependency, SPR days‑cover, stranded/escorted ships.
- Scenario matrix (Deal vs Standoff vs Escalation) with India policy levers.

## Speaker Notes To Build
- Clarify absolute dates: Feb 28 start; Apr 8 conditional ceasefire; Apr 12 blockade; May 4 escorts.
- Emphasize “historic” energy shock language from IEA and why India’s high dependency/SPR profile matters.
- Detail India’s actionable controls: convoy participation windows; sanctions compliance checklists; SPR draw/ refill logic; hedging; alternative sourcing and scheduling.
</PRESENTATION_OUTLINE>
